title: How I Mentored 180+ Students!
description: Led Production and QA student mentorship for 180+ students across 12 teams through structured worfklows, open retrospectives, and technical education.

body: As a **Production & QA Student Mentor**, I supported over **180 interdisciplinary students** across 12 game teams by providing structured guidance on production workflows, QA practices, and delivery readiness.

In addition to one-on-one mentorship, I led multiple breakout sessions and presentations to establish shared production standards, encourage open discussion, and improve technical awareness across teams.

This presentation introduces students to the role of a QA Advocate and the importance of structured quality assurance within a game development pipeline. It covers core QA workflows such as build checklists, milestone checklists, bug reporting, and test management, emphasizing QA as a collaborative, cross-disciplinary responsibility rather than an isolated role.

The talk also explores how QA data directly supports production decision making, including build readiness, task prioritization, and risk assessment. Through concrete examples, templates, and workflows, students learn how clear reporting, consistent testing practices, and open communication contribute to healthier development cycles and higher-quality builds.

blocks.caption:: Optimization Breakout to 15+ Students in Unreal Engine!

body: This talk was designed to help student teams identify, diagnose, and prioritize performance issues using Unreal Engine's built in optimization tools.

The presentation covers rasterization cost, screen space impact, LODs and impostors, lighting and shadow complexity, shader complexity and quad overdraw, material instruction counts, and draw call management. Emphasis is placed on production realistic optimization workflows — teaching teams how to focus on high impact fixes rather than low return micro-optimizations.

The content reflects real production challenges commonly encountered in student and small team projects, bridging the gap between visual quality, performance constraints, and practical development timelines.

items: Mentored Producers, QA advocates, and team leads across **12 teams** on production planning, scope management, and delivery readiness
Led multiple breakout sessions focused on **QA fundamentals**, including build and milestone checklists, bug reporting workflows, and test management practices

Designed and presented a **semester wide retrospective** with all team producers and quality advocates, fostering open discussion in a professor-free environment to promote candid feedback and reflection
Provided structured guidance on **QA workflows**, helping teams adopt consistent testing and reporting standards
Delivered a technical presentation on **Unreal Engine optimization**, covering profiling tools and in-engine art optimization best practices
Reviewed builds, documentation, and team processes to assess project health and identify risks early
A structured, producer driven approach to production and QA education at scale
Clear, actionable frameworks for QA workflows and production checklists
The ability to translate complex technical and process concepts into accessible presentations
An emphasis on open communication, trust, and psychological safety within team discussions
How to scale mentorship through group sessions without sacrificing individual team needs
How shared QA standards significantly improve build stability and team confidence
How safe, open retrospectives can surface critical production insights often missed in formal reviews
How technical education, such as engine optimization knowledge, empowers better cross-disciplinary decision-making

title: Producing from Concept to Publication
description: Learn about how I was the Producer on a 10-person interdisciplinary team for a published work all the way from concept.

body: As **Producer**, I was responsible for guiding Gambler's Fallacy from **early concept** through **playable milestones**.

I oversaw team coordination, **scope management**, and **production planning** while supporting **rapid iteration** across design, art and engineering. My focus was **maintaining momentum** and clarity while enabling the team to **execute creatively** and **efficiently**.

blocks.caption: A screenshot of our Excel task management tool.

items: Defined **production milestones** and **sprint goals** to **guide development** from prototype to polished build
Coordinated **cross-disciplinary** communication between design, art and programming
Managed **scope and feature prioritization** to ensure the project remained **achievable** within time constraints
Facilitated **playtesting** and **feedback sessions** to inform iteration and **production decisions**

blocks.text: Producer Challenges - Team Composition & Role Balance

items: Navigated an imbalanced team structure with eight programmers supporting a single designer and artist
Coordinated responsibilities as some programmers naturally took on design focused tasks to maintain development velocity
Worked to preserve a clear creative vision while allowing flexible role ownership during iteration
Adjusted planning and communication to prevent overlap, misalignment, or decision bottlenecks
Continuity and team stability from **producing multiple projects** with largely the **same team**
An established **production rhythm** that allowed the team to move **quickly during early development**
A **technical background** that helped **bridge communication** between **design** intent and **engineering** constraints
An **adaptive leadership** approach that supported **flexible role ownership** while maintaning overall direction

blocks.text: What I Learned Through This Project

items: Gained a **deeper understanding** of **designer needs** and workflows through my first close collaboration with a dedicated game designer
Learned how to **support and protect design intent** while coordinating implementation across a large programming team
Expanded my **understanding** of **product management** by working closely with a programmer focused on tooling and planning
Developed an appreciation for how **production tools** and **visibility systems** can directly **improve team efficiency** and decision making
Refined my ability to produce for the same team across multiple projects, applying lessons learned from earlier productions

title: Gambler's Fallacy
description: I worked alongside a UI Designer and Artist to implement gameplay interfaces across multiple iterations.

body: On the technical/UI side, I engineered a robust front-end architecture in Unity, working closely with both a UI Designer and UI Artist to deliver flexible, production-ready systems.

I implemented multiple UI architectural patterns, including MVC and MVVM, to cleanly separate data, logic, and presentation across various menu and gameplay interfaces. For high-level navigation, I built a Page Stack Menu Controller, enabling structured screen transitions through push/pop operations similar to modern app navigation stacks.

To support designer-driven workflows, I integrated DOTween to create reusable, designer-friendly animated page transitions, allowing screens to smoothly animate on push/pop without requiring custom scripting for each menu. I also created tools and editor setups that made widget composition, state changes, and layout iteration significantly easier for both the Designer and the Artist.

Implemented a Page/Stack based system to handle in game UI systems, including pop and push callback functions. Additionally, having different animations for menus entering/exiting, with different easing options.
This architecture allows smooth transitions and flexible control over menu flow enabling scalability and proper menu management.
The main menu was implemented to be in world space to give a more diegetic feel — simulating that the player is at a poker table.
Utilizing a button manager to handle all world space animations and button functionality.

blocks.caption: Learn about how I refactored the inventory using a web development pattern.

body: Developed a modular inventory system in Unity using an MVC (Model–View–Controller) architecture.

The Model layer was built on top of another developer's backend inventory system, and I collaborated closely with them to refine and extend the API. This ensured that my View and Controller layers could reliably interface with the underlying data while maintaining clean separation of responsibilities.

On the UI side, I implemented a fully interactive drag-and-drop system for deck management, allowing players to intuitively move cards or items between the deck and various inventory sections. I also built hover-based item tooltips that dynamically display detailed item descriptions and stats, improving clarity and user feedback during inventory interactions.
This architecture-focused approach resulted in a flexible, maintainable inventory UI that could adapt to future features and integrate seamlessly with the backend system.
The credits features two parts, the sliding panels and individual team roles.
Utilizing a menu/page stack system to handle all pushes/pop animations.
Alongside this, the initial credits animation uses a pre-push action to animate the camera and then bring in the credit panels/cards.

items: Collaborated with a UI designer to develop scalable, designer-friendly interfaces.
Engineered an inventory management system using an MVC framework.
Designed and implemented a menu controller with an API supporting a dynamic page/stack system.
Developed a procedural system for UI art to generate multiple interface assets.
It was difficult to handle two roles of being a UI/UX engineer and a producer at once.
Learning how to closely collaborate with a UI designer for the first time, to best support them in the design process
As gameplay systems that needed UI systems, became bigger and needed more attention. Resulting in the UI systems needing to be restructured and rewritten
Learning and implementing new UI architectures such as MVC, MVVM, and a Page stack system, and iterating upon them

title: Chemical Overdrive
description: Developed in Unreal Engine 5 by a collaborative team of 10, including programmers, designers, a composer/sound designer, and a UI artist, Chemical Overdrive delivers a visceral, punk-inspired experience with an aggressive sense of style and speed.

body: On the technical side, I developed a custom UI workflow entirely in UMG and Blueprints. I built a modular widget framework designed for rapid iteration and efficient reuse across multiple menus and HUD elements. A central part of this system was a custom state switcher, a Blueprint-driven mechanism that controlled widget activation, deactivation, and transitions without relying on heavy or monolithic menu blueprints. This switcher abstracted state management into a clean, maintainable structure, making it easy to swap UI screens, update widget states, and manage layered UI flows.

To streamline UI creation, I also built reusable modular button components, each exposing customizable styling, animations, interaction logic, and sound cues. This allowed the UI team to rapidly assemble screens with consistent function and aesthetics while reducing duplicated logic.

This technical foundation improved iteration speed, UI stability, and menu clarity throughout development, contributing to a polished and responsive front-end experience aligned with the game’s fast-moving, high-energy identity.
Designed and implemented a stylized main menu in Unreal Engine 5 featuring a live, cinematic background.

I created a main menu experience that feels dynamic and alive by incorporating an animated in-engine backdrop, ambient city visuals, and subtle character breathing animations. Paired with atmospheric music, the menu conveys a strong sense of place and mood, giving players an immediate “city-at-night” vibe before the game even begins.
Designed and developed a multi-checkpoint system. having two different kinds of checkpoints for local and major points, throughout the level.
Additionally, implementing a compact multi-widget HUD. To represent health, stamina, and dash cooldown.

items: Collaborated with a UI designer to develop scalable, designer-friendly interfaces.
Engineered an inventory management system using an MVC framework.
Designed and implemented a menu controller with an API supporting a dynamic page/stack system.
Developed a procedural system for UI art to generate multiple interface assets.
Led the design and development of all user interfaces, collaborating with a UI artist to bring in high-fidelity interfaces.
Responsible for implementing and overseeing all user interface feedback, such as checkpoint systems, HUD progress bars, and tutorialization.
Designed and developed a full checkpoint system, with major and minor checkpoints. Aiding it with user interface pop ups
Utilized Unreal Engine's UMG system, to create hierarchal widgets, to manage the HUD and sub widgets
Difficulty managing the responsibilities of being a first time producer and being a UI/UX engineer
As it was my first time using Unreal Engine, and UMG, it was interesting to learn all the nuances of the systems and engine
We initially overscoped, causing a major cut from the project towards the end. I had trouble remaking a roadmap and end vision
Oversaw task management and milestone planning to ensure smooth development flow and on-time deliverables
Facilitated weekly team meetings, set clear agendas, and coordinated cross-discipline communication
Monitored team morale and workload, resolving blockers early and promoting a healthy development environment. Using custom made tools that visualized individual workloads
Acted as the primary point of contact between the design, audio, engineering teams and additionally faculty advisors

The team was resistant to using ClickUp, so I facilitated a transition to a custom Excel based workflow that was made by another team member. Which better matched the team's preferences, resulting in improved participation and clear task visibility
Addressed interpersonal conflicts in the early stages of development by mediating discussions, establishing clearer expectations, and promoting transparent communication
Whenever build or technical problems arose, I ensured quick escalation by coordinating with the appropriate faculty advisors, minimizing downtime nad keeping development on track

Some team members found the updated task and bug tracking workflow disrusptive, so I maintained direct oversight of team tasks and bug reporting, ensuring accurate documentation while allowing members to focus on their core work without friction
